details.text: Derivative: ٱلَّذِى can be translated as “the one who”. It can be specific (خَاصّ - Khaass) here in reference to a specific individual of Quraish or it can be general (عَامّ – ‘Aamm) and be applicable to anyone to whom the description fits.

Derivative: يُكَذِّبُ is from كَذَّبَ – تَكْذِيب means to give the lie to something, to lie against it or to call something a lie or someone a liar. It is commonly translated to mean “to deny” and can refer to the act of refuting the truth of something or of a testimony. تَكْذِيب (Takdheeb) can imply the act of denying something or lying against it whilst knowing or accepting deep inside that it is the truth i.e., a denial of reality.

Derivative: الدِّينِ (Ad-Deen) – Its terminological meaning is that it refers to Islam as a whole i.e., the Deen of Islam and all that its message includes and entails. Its literal linguistic meaning comes from دَاَنَ – يَدينُ which literally means to take a loan and to become indebted. It was used an expression that is related to payback and as such الدِّينِ (Ad-Deen) has been understood to refer to the recompense and being held to account on the Day of Judgement when people will be paid back in full.

Derivative: أَرَءَيْتَ “Have you seen?!” – This phrase is used to arouse wonder i.e., considering the state of such a person being described. “Have you seen?” can mean have you literally seen with your eyes. It can also have a figurative meaning like “Have you considered or reflected upon”. It can also imply: “Have you recognised?”

Derivative: يَدُعُّ from دَعَّ means to push someone away with the expectation that they can’t push back and can resemble the act of bullying by being mean, harsh, forcefully driving someone back or away and repelling him. This verb is also used in Surah At-Tur verse 13 to describe how the deniers will be pushed violently into the hellfire: يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلَىٰ نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا “The day they are pushed violently into the fire of Jahannam”.

Derivative: فَ (Faa’) is used to connect the previous statement الفَاءُ العَاطِفَة (Al-Faa’ Al-‘Aatifah) and can carry the meaning of “then”. It can provide explanation for the first statement by being connected to the beginning of the second statement i.e., Have you seen the one who denies the day of recompense, “then” that is the one who pushes the orphan aside. فَ (Faa’) can also be understood to be الفَاء السَّبَبِيَة (Al-Faa’ As-Sababiyah – A Faa’ that alludes to a cause) which links this فَاء with what came before, making it the reason for what comes after i.e., Because or as a result of denying the Deen, he pushes the orphan away. The two meanings of the الفَاءُ can be brought together to highlight that the act of pushing the orphan away is a typical behaviour of the one mentioned in the first verse who denies the day of recompense.

Derivative: ذَٰلِكَ (that) is used here to refer to the one being described instead of هَذَا (this) in the case where ذَٰلِكَ is used to demonstrate something that is distanced and implies contempt for the one being addressed by it and belittles him.

Derivative: الْيَتِيمَ (Al-Yateem – The orphan) refers to someone who has lost their parents when they were in their childhood. The terminological meaning of الْيَتِيمَ (Al-Yateem – The orphan) is the one who has lost his father before he has reached the age of puberty whilst لَطِيم (Lateem) is used for the one who has lost both parents before puberty.

Derivative: يَحُضُّ comes from حَضَّ which implies to push or stir something along, to urge it to go ahead. It is used for pushing forward plans, agendas or when you want to motivate people to get something done or do something good. It implies urging and encouraging.

Derivative: ٱلْمِسْكِينِ comes from مَسْكَنَة refers to poverty. ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (Al-Miskeen) is the impoverished person who is in need. The terminological definition of ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (Al-Miskeen) is the person who possesses a little but not enough to meet his primary and basic needs.

- طَعَامِ الْمِسْكِينِ literally means “The food of the needy”. It does not say feeding the needy and as such it highlights that the food is a right of the person in need i.e., it implies the food which is actually theirs by right and is being prevented from reaching the person in need. If the word “feeding” (إِطْعَام) was used here instead it would have implied a charity in the case where the person would be feeding others from what is his. However, the use of the word طَعَامِ (Ta’aam – Food) attached to the الْمِسْكِينِ (needy person) means that the food is his by right and that it is not a charity.
Derivative: وَيْلٌ is used here as an expression of condemnation and is a like a supplication for ruin and destruction to befall upon the one whom it is said to.

فَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُصَلِّينَ

- “So (or then), ruin and destruction be due (فَوَيْلٌ) upon those who pray (لِّلْمُصَلِّينَ)”. This condemnation is be addressed towards everyone who prays and as such the statement inspires fear and terror in anyone who prays.
- وَيْلٌ (ruin and destruction) has been ascribed to people in the Qur’an who commit blatant crimes and sinful behaviour but here it is be ascribed to those who pray “لِّلْمُصَلِّينَ” and this conjures the thought of how such a condemnation can be warranted for those who pray, which on face value is a good act.
- It is said that this verse has been said to refer to the hypocrites in Al-Madinah whilst the previous verses were said to refer to some of the leaders of Quraish in Makkah. Another view is that it is a continuation of the speech addressing the leaders of Quraish and could refer to how they corrupted the prayer that was left as a legacy to them by Ibrahim (as).

Derivative: سَاهُونَ is derived from سَهْو (Sahw) which means to forget something out of neglect or inattentiveness. سَاهُونَ refers here to those who are neglectful, lax and heedless i.e., they don’t give something its right or the attention, care and importance that it deserves. In respect to the prayer, it can refer to not praying on time and to not giving the prayer its right in terms of rushing it and not observing its components properly.

- “From the prayer”. Here عَن (‘An – from) is used to indicate distance rather than فِي (Fee – in) which would have meant “in their prayer”. As such it is not specifying forgetfulness during the performance of the prayer but rather that which takes someone away from the true performance of the prayer and the fulfilment of its rights.

Derivative: يُرَاءُونَ from رِئَاء and مُراءَاة which means to make a show before people and to make an effort to be seen to be doing something by others. It implies hypocrisy, the lack of true sincerity and that the action is nothing but an act. It is used to imply showing yourself off as being someone who is good or righteous in contrast to your true self.

Derivative: الْمَاعُونَ comes from مَعْن (Ma’n) which refers to something that is small and insignificant the loss of which would not impact you. It was used to imply simple utensils that people would usually share with each other to assist one another like a bucket, needle or shovel etc. In today’s time it would be like lending someone a pen for a short time to fill out a form. It refers to something that it is inconceivable to be cheap or stingy in respect to giving it. It implies the mentality of the person who can’t stand to do something good for someone without gaining a benefit or advantage for himself from that or just because of the hardness of that person’s heart.
Derivative: يَمْنَعُونَ from مَنَعَ – مَنْعٌ and it means to forbid, prevent and withhold something.
